title: Benchmarking Gaussian Splat Reconstructions: PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS & Test Protocols

learningObjectives.description: Compute PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS for a rendered vs. ground-truth image pair and interpret what each value implies about reconstruction quality
Apply the LLFF 1/8 holdout protocol to split a multi-view capture dataset into training and test sets, and explain why using training views for evaluation inflates reported PSNR
Interpret SOGS compression results by reading a compression ratio vs. quality table, identifying the rate-distortion operating point, and comparing against the 3DGS baseline
Identify common benchmarking pitfalls (training-set evaluation, dataset-specific tuning, missing baseline comparisons) and design a reproducible evaluation protocol that avoids them

content:
## Why Held-Out Test Views Matter

A reconstruction algorithm is only as good as its generalization to *unseen* viewpoints. If you evaluate novel-view synthesis on training images, any method that memorizes the training data achieves perfect scores — a trivially overfitted model would top every leaderboard. The standard protocol therefore withholds a subset of input images from both COLMAP reconstruction and 3DGS training, then evaluates the rendered output against those held-out images.

Three metrics dominate the 3DGS benchmarking literature: PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS. Each captures a different aspect of image quality, and no single metric tells the full story.

## Mean Squared Error: The Foundation

All three metrics begin from the pixel-level difference between a rendered image \(\hat{I}\) and a reference image \(I\). Mean Squared Error measures the average squared per-pixel deviation:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{HWC}\sum_{c=1}^{C}\sum_{i=1}^{H}\sum_{j=1}^{W}\Bigl(I_{c,i,j} - \hat{I}_{c,i,j}\Bigr)^2$$

where \(H, W\) are image height and width, \(C = 3\) for RGB images, and indices \(i, j\) range over spatial positions. MSE is simple and differentiable, making it suitable as a training loss, but it correlates poorly with human perception: a small global brightness shift produces a large MSE, while structured noise that looks terrible can have moderate MSE.

## PSNR: Signal Fidelity in Decibels

PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) converts MSE to a logarithmic scale anchored to the maximum possible signal value:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$$

The numerator \(255^2\) corresponds to the squared peak value for uint8 images (\([0, 255]\) range). Higher PSNR means smaller MSE — less difference between rendered and reference images.

**Interpreting dB values**: A 3 dB increase represents a halving of MSE (since \(10\log_{10}(2) \approx 3\)). Typical 3DGS PSNR values on standard benchmarks fall in the **27–33 dB range**, with SOGS compression introducing losses of **0.2–1.0 dB** depending on compression ratio. Differences below ~1 dB are generally imperceptible to human observers.

PSNR is the most widely reported metric in novel-view synthesis papers because it is simple to compute and straightforward to compare. Its main limitation: it treats all pixel errors as equally important, ignoring spatial structure and perceptual salience.

## SSIM: Structural Similarity

SSIM (Structural Similarity Index) measures perceptual similarity by comparing three statistics computed over local windows \(x, y\) of the rendered and reference images:

$$\text{SSIM}(x, y) = \frac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + c_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + c_2)}$$

where \(\mu_x, \mu_y\) are local means, \(\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2\) are local variances, \(\sigma_{xy}\) is the local covariance, and \(c_1, c_2\) are small stabilizing constants. The index ranges in \([-1, 1]\), with 1 indicating identical images and values above **0.85** generally considered visually acceptable for 3DGS outputs.

SSIM is more robust than PSNR to low-frequency brightness and contrast variations, but it uses fixed 11×11 pixel windows — making it insensitive to structural differences at other scales.

## LPIPS: Learned Perceptual Distance

LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity) computes the \(\ell_2\) distance between deep feature maps extracted by a pretrained VGG or AlexNet network. Unlike PSNR and SSIM, LPIPS is trained to match human perceptual judgements of image similarity.

**Lower LPIPS is better** (it measures distance, not similarity). Typical 3DGS LPIPS values are in the **0.08–0.25** range. LPIPS is especially sensitive to ghosting, blur, and hallucinated details — failure modes that PSNR and SSIM can underestimate. For SOGS compression, LPIPS often degrades less than PSNR suggests: SOM-ordered quantization preserves structural coherence even as per-pixel MSE increases slightly.

## The LLFF 1/8 Holdout Protocol

The Local Light Field Fusion (LLFF) dataset introduced the **1/8 holdout**: every 8th image is withheld as a test view, and the remaining 7/8 are used for reconstruction and training. This protocol became the standard for forward-facing scenes because:

1. **Uniform coverage**: systematic sampling ensures test views are distributed across the entire camera trajectory, not clustered in any region.
2. **Reproducibility**: the holdout set is deterministic — identical across papers, enabling fair comparison.
3. **Interpolation vs. extrapolation balance**: every test view has training neighbors within 7 frames on either side, making evaluation neither trivially easy (direct neighbor interpolation) nor unrealistically hard (extreme viewpoint extrapolation).

For a 1,179-frame video at 8 fps, the 1/8 holdout yields approximately **147 test images** and **1,032 training images**.

## Standard Benchmark Datasets

Three datasets appear in virtually every 3DGS compression paper:

| Dataset | Scenes | Type | Resolution | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mip-NeRF 360 | 9 | Indoor + outdoor, 360° unbounded | 1.4–7.8 MP | Gold standard for unbounded reconstruction |
| Tanks and Temples | 2 (Train, Truck) | Outdoor, large-scale | 2 MP | Tests reconstruction under challenging lighting |
| Deep Blending | 2 (DrJohnson, Playroom) | Indoor, high-baseline | 1.4 MP | Tests close-range reconstruction with occlusions |

The original 3DGS paper (Kerbl et al. 2023) reports results on all three. SOGS benchmarks typically focus on Tanks and Temples as a representative medium-scale outdoor scene — the same dataset used for the `SOGS_Pipeline.ipynb` reference notebook.

## Interpreting SOGS vs. 3DGS Results

When comparing SOGS to standard 3DGS at the same scene, look for:

**File size reduction**: The primary SOGS benefit. Standard 3DGS `.ply` files are 200–500 MB for typical scenes. SOGS compression targets **10–30× reduction** while staying within 1 dB PSNR of the uncompressed baseline.

**PSNR vs. compression tradeoff curve**: A well-behaved compressor maintains high PSNR at moderate compression and degrades gracefully as compression increases. A poor compressor loses PSNR rapidly even at modest compression ratios.

**LPIPS as a quality floor**: Even if PSNR drops 0.5 dB under compression, LPIPS should remain stable if the SOM ordering has preserved structural coherence. A sudden LPIPS jump at some compression ratio signals a phase transition — the quantization has begun to destroy structure, not just add noise.

**Timing**: SOGS training adds SOM+PLAS runtime on top of 3DGS training. On a T4 GPU, the SOM phase typically adds 15–30 minutes to the pipeline. This tradeoff is acceptable for deployment scenarios where storage size dominates inference cost.

terms.name: Normalisation factor
terms.desc: Divides by the total number of scalar values in the image (height × width × channels). Ensures MSE is comparable across images of different resolutions. For a 1280×720 RGB image, HWC = 2,764,800.

terms.name: Sum over all pixels and channels
terms.desc: Accumulates the squared error at every spatial position (i,j) and colour channel c. Each term is non-negative, so MSE can only increase as more pixels differ — there is no cancellation between positive and negative errors.

terms.name: Squared pixel difference
terms.desc: Squaring penalises large errors disproportionately (a pixel off by 10 counts 100× more than one off by 1). This makes MSE sensitive to outlier errors — a few badly reconstructed pixels dominate the metric, even if most of the image is perfect.

terms.name: Squared peak signal value
terms.desc: The maximum possible squared pixel value for uint8 images. This anchors the ratio: when MSE → 0 (perfect reconstruction), PSNR → ∞. When MSE = 255² (every pixel maximally wrong), PSNR = 0 dB.

terms.name: Decibel conversion
terms.desc: Converts the linear ratio to a logarithmic scale in decibels. Each +3 dB corresponds to halving the MSE; each +10 dB corresponds to a 10× reduction. This scale matches human perception more closely than the linear MSE ratio.

terms.name: Mean Squared Error
terms.desc: The denominator in the ratio. A perfect image has MSE = 0, making PSNR undefined (infinite). In practice, any two distinct images have MSE > 0. PSNR is typically measured on float images converted to uint8 range [0, 255] before comparison.

references.label: Kerbl et al. 2023
references.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering

references.label: Wang et al. 2004
references.title: Image Quality Assessment: From Error Visibility to Structural Similarity

references.label: Zhang et al. 2018
references.title: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Deep Features as a Perceptual Metric (LPIPS)
